chemistry.scope_summary: Various 4-hydroxycyclobutenones with alkyl, aryl, alkynyl, and heteroaryl substituents (1b-h) underwent smooth rearrangement to corresponding furanones in excellent yields. The 2-pyridyl substrate 1i gave a 1:1 mixture of furanone 4i and quinolizinonе 5.

optimization_table.key_findings: Switch from THF to acetonitrile as solvent gave step-change improvement; UVA lamp gave clean conversion

key_observations_supporting_mechanism: different stereochemical outcomes for thermal vs photochemical pathways
figures.caption: Scheme 1. Thermal and photochemical rearrangements of 4-hydroxy-cyclobutenone 1.

figures.flow_path: reactant solution in → product solution out, with cooling water in/out and cool air in/warm air out
figures.key_information: Photochemical reactor with interchangeable low-energy 9W lamp for various wavelengths, double coiled PFA tubing around quartz tube lamp housing, with cooling system
figures.caption: Figure 1. Flow photochemical reactor (cut-away between grey lines). PFA = perfluoroalkoxy.
Scheme 2. Photochemically induced cyclobutenone rearrangements of 1a–h [in a flask] and under continuous flow.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low yield (27%) in photochemical batch conditions
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Improved control of residence time and temperature enables higher yields
translation_logic.what_changed: Reactor design (coiled PFA tubing with integrated lamp), solvent switch to acetonitrile, precise temperature control (100°C), optimized residence time (120 min)
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Same starting materials and products, photochemical activation mechanism
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Continuous flow eliminates need for large quantities of heated solvent under photochemical conditions

figure_descriptions: Detailed reactor cutaway showing PFA coil around quartz tube lamp housing with cooling system
Mechanistic schemes showing thermal vs photochemical pathways
Substrate scope table with yields for 8 examples

conflicts: Reactor volume stated as 18 mL in text but calculated from 24m × 1mm ID tubing gives ~18.8 mL
notes: Well-documented flow photochemistry paper with clear reactor design and optimization data. Missing some quantitative details like exact flow rates for individual streams and back pressure values. Yield improvement from batch to flow is substantial (27% to 54% for initial conditions, up to 97% after optimization).
